title: WORD Motions
subtitle: {key:W}, {key:B}, {key:E}, {key:gE} — like {key:w}/{key:b}/{key:e}/{key:ge} but bigger words.
summary: Capital-letter word motions treat any non-whitespace character as part of the word. So foo->bar.baz is one WORD, three words.

blocks.text: {key:w} skips between *keyword* runs. {key:W} skips between *whitespace*-separated chunks. The capital is bigger.

blocks.label: Back to end of previous WORD

blocks.caption: W / B / E / gE — WORD motions

blocks.title: Word vs WORD
blocks.text:
**word** ({key:w} family) — bounded by `'iskeyword'` (default: letters, digits, underscore). In `foo->bar->baz`, the words are `foo`, `->`, `bar`, `->`, `baz`. Five words, lots of stops.

**WORD** ({key:W} family) — bounded by *whitespace only*. Same string is one WORD.

Which do you want? Depends on the task:

  • Editing inside an identifier with operators? word.
  • Skipping past a URL or a long quoted argument? WORD.
  • Operating on a complete shell-style argument? WORD.

There's a matching pair of text objects too: {key:i}{key:W} / {key:a}{key:W}. {key:c}{key:i}{key:W} on a long URL changes the whole URL; {key:c}{key:i}{key:w} would change just one segment of it.
